qa_pairs.question: What is considered one of the cornerstones of application development?
qa_pairs.answer: Source code management is one of the cornerstones of application development.

qa_pairs.question: What are the two general ways to proceed when managing a project's source code?
qa_pairs.answer: You could make multiple backup copies, send the source code to your colleagues by email, and waste time trying to remember who has or is using which version, or you could choose a much more rational approach by using a revision control system.

qa_pairs.question: Why are revision control tools particularly important for academic research?
qa_pairs.answer: For academic research, these tools are even more important because traceability is essential for ensuring that a given set of results can be reproduced.

qa_pairs.question: What metaphor is used to describe revision control management tools for programmers?
qa_pairs.answer: A good metaphor is that revision control management tools are the programmer's equivalent to the experimentalist's lab notebook.

qa_pairs.question: What is an advantage of revision control tools related to collaboration?
qa_pairs.answer: They permit you to collaborate more easily and eliminate the risk that a collaborator might delete your modifications or vice versa without leaving a trace.

qa_pairs.question: How do revision control tools function like a 'time machine'?
qa_pairs.answer: These tools save the history of all the modifications made to a project and in that way function somewhat like a time machine, allowing you to reinitialize your project to an earlier version.

qa_pairs.question: What basic principle do source code management tools use?
qa_pairs.answer: Source code management tools function using a basic principle of separating local modifications made by a user, in his or her local directory, and what is called the repository.

qa_pairs.question: What information does the repository contain in a source code management system?
qa_pairs.answer: The repository contains, in a structured manner, the history of all of the modifications made by all of a project's contributors.

qa_pairs.question: What action must a contributor perform to make their modifications available to other developers?
qa_pairs.answer: You as a contributor have to submit (commit) your modifications to the repository in order to make them available to other developers.

qa_pairs.question: What must developers do to ensure they are using the latest version of a file before making their own modifications?
qa_pairs.answer: Developers need to make sure they are using the latest version of a file by retrieving it from the repository (checkout, update) before making their own modifications.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if two programmers modify the same source code file simultaneously?
qa_pairs.answer: If two programmers modify the same source code file at the same time, the source code management tool may report a conflict during the submission of the two rival modifications or automatically resolve the conflict if possible.

qa_pairs.question: How are the principal revision control tools categorized?
qa_pairs.answer: The principal revision control tools can be divided into two families or generations.

qa_pairs.question: What is a characteristic feature of first-generation revision control tools?
qa_pairs.answer: First-generation tools, which include CVS and SVN, use a single central repository.

qa_pairs.question: What is an advantage of second-generation revision control tools that use local repositories?
qa_pairs.answer: The advantage of such a system is that development work can be done independent of any remote server.

qa_pairs.question: Name three examples of second-generation revision control tools.
qa_pairs.answer: Second-generation tools include Git, Mercurial, and Bazaar.

qa_pairs.question: What advanced management feature do Git and Mercurial offer?
qa_pairs.answer: Git and Mercurial offer advanced management of branched development.

qa_pairs.question: What does the 'production' version of a project correspond to in a branched development model?
qa_pairs.answer: The production version of the project then corresponds to the principal branch.

qa_pairs.question: For second-generation tools, what are the two steps for modifications to go to an external repository?
qa_pairs.answer: First, they are submitted to the local repository (commit), then they are pushed (push) to the external repository.

qa_pairs.question: For second-generation tools, what are the two steps to retrieve modifications from an external repository?
qa_pairs.answer: You must first obtain them (pull or get) so they can be imported into the local repository, then update your working version (update or checkout).

qa_pairs.question: What determines the choice of a version control tool if you are contributing to an existing project?
qa_pairs.answer: If you want to contribute to an existing project, you don't really have any choice; you will have to use the tool that has been chosen by the initial development team.

qa_pairs.question: Which type of revision control tool is sufficient for a new, private project with a few contributors needing only modification history?
qa_pairs.answer: A first-generation tool like SVN can be sufficient for a project with only a few contributors, which will remain private and for which you would simply like to have a history of all the modifications.

qa_pairs.question: What type of tool should be considered for a larger project involving external collaborators?
qa_pairs.answer: If your project is larger, with external collaborators, you should consider a second-generation tool like Git or Mercurial.
